text: HB 1234: An Act Concerning Security Deposit Limits. This bill would cap security deposits at one month's rent for all residential rental properties and require landlords to return deposits within 21 days of lease termination, with itemized deductions.

SB 567: Eviction Moratorium Extension. This bill extends the current eviction moratorium for residential tenants through December 2026. Landlords are prohibited from filing eviction notices for nonpayment of rent during the moratorium period.

Ordinance 2026-42: Rental Property Registration Program. The City Council hereby establishes a mandatory rental property registration program. All owners of residential rental property within city limits must register each unit annually and pay a $50 per-unit registration fee. Properties must pass inspection every three years.

Resolution 2026-15: Source of Income Discrimination Prohibition. It shall be unlawful for any landlord, property manager, or real estate broker to refuse to rent, or to discriminate in the terms of rental, based on a prospective tenant's lawful source of income, including housing choice vouchers (Section 8), social security, disability payments, or child support.

AB 890: Tenant Screening and Application Fee Reform. This bill limits rental application fees to $30, requires landlords to provide applicants with a copy of any screening report, and prohibits landlords from charging application fees if no unit is available.

SB 1122: Rent Stabilization Act. This bill establishes rent increase caps of 5% plus CPI annually for residential properties older than 15 years. Landlords must provide 90 days notice before any rent increase and may not increase rent more than once per 12-month period.

Municipal Code Amendment: Habitability Standards Update. The Building Department shall require that all rental dwelling units maintain minimum habitability standards including working plumbing, electrical, heating systems, and structural integrity. Landlords must address habitability complaints within 14 days or face penalties of $100 per day.

HUD Final Rule: Affirmatively Furthering Fair Housing. The Department of Housing and Urban Development issues this final rule reinstating the obligation of jurisdictions receiving federal housing funds to affirmatively further fair housing. This rule requires jurisdictions to conduct fair housing assessments and develop plans to address discriminatory housing patterns.

HB 445: Just Cause Eviction Requirements. This bill requires landlords to provide just cause for eviction of residential tenants who have occupied a unit for more than 12 months. Allowable causes include nonpayment of rent, lease violations, owner move-in, and substantial renovation. Relocation assistance of one month's rent is required for no-fault evictions.

County Ordinance: Background Check Restrictions for Housing. Landlords and property managers are prohibited from considering arrests that did not result in conviction, convictions older than 7 years, or juvenile records when evaluating rental applications. Criminal history inquiries may not be made until after a conditional offer of tenancy.

SB 789: Landlord-Tenant Code Modernization. This comprehensive bill updates the state's landlord-tenant code to require electronic lease options, establish minimum notice periods of 60 days for lease non-renewals, mandate interest on security deposits, and create a state tenant hotline.

Federal Register Notice: CFPB Proposed Rule on Tenant Screening Reports. The Consumer Financial Protection Bureau proposes new regulations on tenant screening reports that would require screening companies to provide dispute resolution processes, limit the types of information reportable, and ensure accuracy of records used in tenant screening decisions.

City Council Meeting Minutes: Discussion of Rent Control Ballot Measure. Council members debated placing a rent control measure on the November ballot. The proposed measure would cap annual rent increases at 3% for buildings older than 20 years. Landlord representatives argued the cap would discourage maintenance investment.

Court Ruling: Smith v. ABC Property Management. The appellate court ruled that a landlord's refusal to accept Housing Choice Vouchers constitutes source-of-income discrimination under the state's fair housing act. The court awarded damages to the plaintiff and ordered the property management company to adopt voucher-acceptance policies.

Executive Order: Federal Eviction Prevention Initiative. The President signed an executive order directing HUD and the Department of Justice to coordinate eviction prevention efforts, establish an eviction diversion grant program, and create a federal database of eviction filings to track displacement patterns.

HB 5678: State Highway Improvement Fund. This bill allocates $2.3 billion for highway and bridge improvements across the state. The funds will be distributed to counties based on road-miles and traffic volume. Projects include resurfacing I-70, replacing the Main Street bridge, and expanding Highway 36.

City Council Meeting Minutes: Library Budget Approval. The council approved the FY2027 library operating budget of $4.2 million. Discussion items included extending Saturday hours at the West Branch, purchasing new children's book collections, and upgrading the computer lab at the Main Branch.

SB 234: School Funding Formula Reform. This bill modifies the state's school funding formula to increase per-pupil funding for districts with high percentages of students eligible for free and reduced-price lunch. The bill also establishes a grant program for school facility improvements.

Ordinance 2026-18: Water and Sewer Rate Adjustment. The City Council approves a 3.5% increase in water rates and a 2.8% increase in sewer rates effective July 1, 2026. The rate increases are necessary to fund infrastructure improvements to the wastewater treatment plant and water distribution system.

Resolution: Sister City Agreement with Nagoya, Japan. The City Council authorizes the Mayor to enter into a sister city agreement with Nagoya, Japan to promote cultural exchange, educational opportunities, and economic development partnerships between the two cities.

HB 9012: Agricultural Tax Credit Extension. This bill extends the existing agricultural property tax credit for five additional years. Qualifying agricultural operations receive a 15% reduction in property tax assessments. The bill also creates a new beginning farmer tax credit.

City Council Meeting Minutes: Parks and Recreation Master Plan. The council received a presentation on the 10-year Parks and Recreation Master Plan. Key recommendations include building two new community centers, adding 50 miles of trails, renovating the municipal pool, and creating three new dog parks.

Federal Register Notice: EPA Proposed Rule on Vehicle Emissions Standards. The Environmental Protection Agency proposes new tailpipe emissions standards for passenger vehicles model years 2028-2032 that would require automakers to achieve fleet-average emissions reductions of 40% compared to 2026 levels.

SB 111: State Employee Pension Reform. This bill modifies the state employee retirement system by increasing the employer contribution rate, adjusting the cost-of-living adjustment formula, and raising the minimum retirement age for new employees hired after January 1, 2027.

County Commission Minutes: Emergency Services Consolidation. The commission voted to consolidate the county's three separate fire districts into a single countywide fire protection district. The consolidation is expected to improve response times and save $1.2 million annually in administrative costs.

HB 3333: Renewable Energy Portfolio Standard. This bill requires electric utilities to generate at least 50% of their electricity from renewable sources by 2035. The bill establishes a renewable energy credit trading system and provides incentives for solar and wind energy development.

City Council Meeting: Approval of New Community Center. The council voted unanimously to approve construction of a new 25,000 square foot community center in the Riverside neighborhood. The $8.5 million project will include a gymnasium, meeting rooms, a commercial kitchen, and outdoor recreation areas.

Federal Register: FDA Rule on Nutritional Labeling. The Food and Drug Administration finalizes a rule requiring restaurants and similar retail food establishments with 20 or more locations to provide calorie information on menus and menu boards. The rule also requires additional nutritional information upon request.

Resolution: National Night Out Proclamation. The Mayor proclaims August 6, 2026 as National Night Out in the City. Residents are encouraged to participate in neighborhood block parties, cookouts, and community-building activities to promote police-community partnerships and crime prevention.

SB 456: Cannabis Regulation and Taxation. This bill establishes a framework for the licensing, regulation, and taxation of recreational cannabis. The bill sets a 15% excise tax, creates a Cannabis Regulatory Commission, and designates tax revenue for public education and substance abuse treatment programs.

City Council Meeting: Budget hearing including allocation of $500,000 for affordable housing programs. Discussion of general fund expenditures including police, fire, and parks. Council also discussed zoning changes for a commercial district and approved a new traffic signal installation.

HB 7777: Comprehensive Housing Omnibus Bill. Among 47 provisions, Section 23 modifies the definition of 'habitable condition' for rental properties to include working air conditioning, and Section 31 requires landlords to accept at least one form of electronic rent payment.

Court Ruling: City of Denver v. National Apartment Association. The court upheld Denver's short-term rental licensing ordinance, finding that the city has authority to regulate rental properties for public health and safety. The ruling does not affect long-term residential rental regulations.

Federal Register: HUD Notice of Funding Opportunity for Housing Counseling. HUD announces $50 million in grants for housing counseling agencies. Eligible activities include pre-purchase counseling, foreclosure prevention, rental housing counseling, and fair housing education. Applications due September 30, 2026.

State Legislature: Amendment to Property Code Section 92.331 extending the deadline for landlords to install smoke detectors in all rental units from 30 days to 14 days after receiving notice of a non-functioning detector. Penalties for non-compliance increased from $500 to $1,000.
